subtopic_detail: Mild diabetic foot infection outpatient management
stem: A 62-year-old man with type 2 diabetes for 12 years presents to his GP with a 3-day history of a warm, mildly painful 2 cm ulcer over the second metatarsal head with surrounding erythema extending 1 cm from the wound. He has no fever, no streaking lymphangitis and a palpable dorsalis pedis pulse. There is no probe-to-bone and no exposed tendon or capsule. He has no penicillin allergy and his HbA1c is 7.2 percent. He has full ankle range of motion.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 132/82, HR 80, Temp 36.9 degrees Celsius, SpO2 99% on room air
data_table.Examination: 2 cm shallow plantar ulcer; surrounding cellulitis 1 cm wide; pedal pulses palpable; no probe-to-bone; sensation diminished to 10 g monofilament at first metatarsal head bilaterally
data_table.Investigations: WCC 8.4 x10^9/L, CRP 18 mg/L, HbA1c 7.2%, creatinine 88 micromol/L, plain foot X-ray negative for osteomyelitis

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate antimicrobial regimen?

options.text: Oral flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 7 days, daily dressing changes, off-loading and review at 5 to 7 days with high-risk foot service input as needed
options.rationale: Mild diabetic foot infection in a non-allergic patient is treated with narrow-spectrum oral antistaphylococcal antibiotics; flucloxacillin is AU first-line. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: diabetic foot infection)

options.text: Intravenous piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g six hourly for 7 days in hospital with vascular and orthopaedic review on admission to manage anaerobic and Pseudomonas cover today
options.rationale: Mild diabetic foot infection without systemic features does not require IV broad-spectrum therapy or admission. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: diabetic foot infection)

options.text: Topical mupirocin 2 percent ointment three times daily for 7 days without any systemic antibiotic therapy initiated at this presentation in the practice today
options.rationale: Surrounding cellulitis indicates a need for systemic, not topical, antibiotics in diabetic foot infection. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: diabetic foot infection)

options.text: Oral metronidazole 400 mg three times daily for 7 days as the sole antibiotic agent to cover anaerobic skin flora colonising the diabetic ulcer base today
options.rationale: Anaerobic monotherapy misses the dominant Staphylococcus aureus and Streptococcus species in mild infections. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: diabetic foot infection)

options.text: Oral cefalexin 500 mg four times daily for 28 days plus oral clindamycin 450 mg eight hourly for 28 days for a prolonged course of dual-agent therapy
options.rationale: Mild infections require around 7 days of treatment; prolonged dual therapy is reserved for osteomyelitis or moderate-severe infection. (Source: eTG Antibiotic: diabetic foot infection)

explanation.summary: Mild diabetic foot infection (cellulitis under 2 cm wide, no systemic features) is treated with oral narrow-spectrum antistaphylococcal antibiotics. Flucloxacillin 500 mg four times daily for 7 days is AU first-line; cefalexin substitutes in non-severe penicillin allergy. Concurrent off-loading, sharp debridement, dressings and glycaemic optimisation are essential. Pseudomonas cover is not required in mild infection. Probe-to-bone or exposed bone prompts MRI and osteomyelitis pathway.
key_points: Flucloxacillin 500 mg QID for 7 days is AU first-line for mild diabetic foot infection
Off-loading and debridement are as important as antibiotics in foot infection
Probe-to-bone or positive bone exposure triggers osteomyelitis workup with MRI
explanation.pearls: Probe-to-bone positivity in a diabetic foot ulcer has a positive predictive value above 80 percent for underlying osteomyelitis and prompts MRI rather than plain film alone.

stem: A 38-year-old asymptomatic woman attends for her routine 5-yearly cervical screening test. Her result returns HPV 16 detected, with reflex liquid-based cytology showing low-grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (LSIL). She has no abnormal bleeding, no postcoital bleeding and is in a monogamous relationship. She has had two normal-result smears in the past 10 years. She is not pregnant and not on any immunosuppression at present.

data_table.Examination: Speculum examination at the time of screening was unremarkable; cervix appeared normal macroscopically
data_table.Investigations: Cervical screening test: HPV 16 detected; reflex LBC: LSIL; previous CSTs at 28 and 33 years HPV not detected with negative cytology; no immunosuppression

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next step?

options.text: Repeat the human papillomavirus test in 12 months and refer to colposcopy only if the same oncogenic type persists at the 12-month follow-up assessment
options.rationale: HPV 16 or 18 mandates direct colposcopy referral regardless of cytology grade; 12-month repeat is for non-16/18 oncogenic types with negative or low-grade cytology. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program guidelines)

options.text: Refer directly to colposcopy for assessment, given oncogenic HPV type 16 detection regardless of the cytology grade reported on the reflex liquid-based cytology
options.rationale: Direct colposcopy referral is the AU pathway for HPV 16 or 18 detection on the CST irrespective of cytology grade. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program guidelines)

options.text: Recommend hysterectomy as a definitive treatment to remove the risk of progression to invasive cervical cancer in this woman with a high-risk HPV type detected today
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is not indicated based on an HPV-positive screen with LSIL cytology; colposcopy and targeted treatment of any high-grade lesion is appropriate. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program guidelines)

options.text: Offer adult human papillomavirus vaccination as the sole intervention and rebook a screening test in 5 years rather than referral to colposcopy at this time
options.rationale: Vaccination does not treat existing infection; it does not replace colposcopy after HPV 16 detection. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program guidelines)

options.text: Repeat the cervical screening test in 5 years as per a routine asymptomatic-woman recall interval since she has no symptoms today in primary care
options.rationale: Routine 5-year recall applies only to HPV-negative screens; HPV 16 detection alters management. (Source: National Cervical Screening Program guidelines)

explanation.summary: Australian cervical screening detects oncogenic HPV by PCR with reflex cytology when HPV is positive. HPV 16 or 18 detection prompts direct colposcopy referral regardless of cytology grade. Non-16/18 oncogenic types with negative or low-grade cytology repeat at 12 months. Persistent infection at 12 months or any high-grade cytology triggers colposcopy. Immunocompromised women have a tighter algorithm.
key_points: HPV 16 or 18 detection on CST means direct colposcopy regardless of cytology
Non-16/18 oncogenic HPV with negative or LSIL cytology returns in 12 months
Vaccination does not treat established HPV infection
explanation.pearls: HPV 16 alone causes around half of invasive cervical cancers worldwide, justifying the direct-to-colposcopy pathway whenever it is detected on a screening test.

sources.label: Cancer Council Australia: cervical cancer screening

subtopic_detail: Anti-D prophylaxis in Rhesus D-negative women
stem: A 26-year-old G1P0 at 27+5 weeks attends her midwifery appointment. Her booking blood group is O Rhesus D negative and her antibody screen at booking was negative for atypical red cell antibodies. The pregnancy is uncomplicated, the fetus is singleton, and there have been no sensitising events such as bleeding, abdominal trauma or procedures. She has no relevant medical history, takes only folate and iodine, and has had no antenatal injections so far.

data_table.Examination: Symphyseal-fundal height appropriate at 28 cm; fetal heart rate 142 by Doppler
data_table.Investigations: Booking: blood group O RhD negative, antibody screen negative, repeat antibody screen at 28 weeks pending; partner blood group unknown; no sensitising events documented this pregnancy

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate plan for anti-D prophylaxis?

options.text: Withhold routine anti-D until a sensitising event occurs or fetal RhD status is established as positive, since prophylaxis is not standard practice in Australian antenatal care
options.rationale: Routine antenatal anti-D at 28 and 34 weeks is standard AU practice in RhD-negative non-sensitised women. (Source: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin)
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

options.text: Administer a single dose of anti-D immunoglobulin 625 IU intramuscular at 36 weeks gestation only, with no second dose given before the postpartum cord-blood test result
options.rationale: AU practice uses 625 IU at both 28 and 34 weeks, not a single late-pregnancy dose. (Source: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin)
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

options.text: Administer anti-D immunoglobulin 625 IU intramuscular now at around 28 weeks and again at around 34 weeks, with a postpartum dose dependent on the cord blood RhD result
options.rationale: AU NBA-funded antenatal prophylaxis is two doses of 625 IU at 28 and 34 weeks plus postpartum based on cord RhD typing. (Source: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin)
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

options.text: Administer two doses of anti-D immunoglobulin 250 IU intramuscular at 28 and 34 weeks gestation as the recommended Australian antenatal prophylaxis dosing in non-sensitised women
options.rationale: The AU antenatal dose is 625 IU, not 250 IU; 250 IU is the early-pregnancy sensitising-event dose under 12 weeks. (Source: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin)
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

options.text: Send a Kleihauer-Betke test today and only administer anti-D immunoglobulin if there is evidence of fetomaternal haemorrhage above the 4 mL fetal red cell threshold today
options.rationale: Kleihauer-Betke is used after a sensitising event to estimate the dose; it is not used to decide on routine antenatal prophylaxis. (Source: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin)
source_refs: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

explanation.summary: Routine antenatal anti-D prophylaxis in RhD-negative non-sensitised women in Australia comprises 625 IU intramuscular at 28 and 34 weeks, plus a postpartum dose if the cord blood is RhD positive. Sensitising events (bleeding, trauma, procedures, ECV) below 12 weeks use 250 IU; above 12 weeks use 625 IU plus Kleihauer-Betke to estimate fetomaternal haemorrhage and any top-up. Cell-free fetal DNA RhD typing is increasingly available but not yet universal.
key_points: Routine antenatal anti-D in Australia is 625 IU at 28 and 34 weeks
Sensitising events under 12 weeks use 250 IU; over 12 weeks use 625 IU plus Kleihauer-Betke
Postpartum dose follows cord blood RhD status of the baby
explanation.pearls: Anti-D 625 IU intramuscular within 72 hours of a sensitising event in a RhD-negative woman reduces alloimmunisation risk from around 16 percent to under 1 percent per pregnancy.

sources.label: NBA Guidelines on the use of Rh(D) immunoglobulin

subtopic_detail: Recurrent miscarriage workup after three first-trimester losses
stem: A 33-year-old G3P0 has had three consecutive first-trimester miscarriages between 7 and 10 weeks, the most recent 8 weeks ago. She is otherwise well with regular cycles and no thrombotic history. Her body mass index is 23 and she does not smoke. Her partner has fathered no children. She and her partner are keen to optimise her chance of a future ongoing pregnancy and ask about evidence-based investigation before they try again.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen; secondary sexual characteristics normal; cervical os closed
data_table.Investigations: TSH 1.6 mU/L, HbA1c 5.1%, FBC normal, midluteal progesterone consistent with ovulation; cycle 28 days; no prior thromboembolism

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate next investigation?

options.text: Request a hysterosalpingogram to assess tubal patency as the priority investigation after a third first-trimester pregnancy loss in this woman before further blood work
options.rationale: Tubal patency assessment does not explain recurrent miscarriage; it is a fertility investigation, not a miscarriage workup. (Source: RANZCOG recurrent miscarriage statement)

options.text: Refer for assisted reproductive technology including in vitro fertilisation with pre-implantation genetic testing as the first-line approach for recurrent miscarriage workup today
options.rationale: IVF and PGT-A do not improve live-birth rates over expectant management in idiopathic recurrent miscarriage and are reserved for specific scenarios. (Source: RANZCOG recurrent miscarriage statement)

options.text: Empirically commence low-dose aspirin 100 mg daily preconception and during early pregnancy without any further targeted laboratory investigations before a fourth pregnancy
options.rationale: Aspirin without an APS diagnosis does not improve outcomes in unexplained recurrent miscarriage; targeted investigation should precede therapy. (Source: RANZCOG recurrent miscarriage statement)

options.text: Test for antiphospholipid antibodies (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin and anti-beta2-glycoprotein I), with parental karyotype if products of conception cytogenetics suggest a parental rearrangement
options.rationale: APS testing is the single most actionable workup; APS detection enables aspirin plus LMWH which improves live-birth rate. Parental karyotyping is targeted by POC findings. (Source: RANZCOG recurrent miscarriage statement)

options.text: Send a thrombophilia panel including factor V Leiden, prothrombin gene mutation and protein C/S as the first-line investigation in this woman with no thrombotic history this presentation
options.rationale: Routine inherited thrombophilia testing in early-pregnancy loss without VTE history is no longer recommended; treatment does not improve live-birth rate. (Source: RANZCOG recurrent miscarriage statement)

explanation.summary: Recurrent miscarriage workup after three first-trimester losses prioritises antiphospholipid antibody testing (lupus anticoagulant, anticardiolipin, anti-beta2-glycoprotein I) twice 12 weeks apart, parental karyotyping when indicated by products of conception, and uterine cavity assessment for structural anomaly. Inherited thrombophilia panels and immunological workup are not routinely recommended. APS treatment with low-dose aspirin plus LMWH from a positive pregnancy test improves live-birth rate.
key_points: APS testing (LAC, ACL, anti-beta2-GP1) is the single most actionable workup in recurrent miscarriage
Inherited thrombophilia testing without VTE history is not routinely recommended
APS-positive women receive low-dose aspirin plus LMWH from positive pregnancy test
explanation.pearls: Antiphospholipid antibodies must be persistently positive 12 weeks apart to meet APS criteria; treatment of confirmed APS with aspirin plus prophylactic LMWH improves live-birth rate from around 40 to 70 percent.

stem: A 64-year-old man presents to ED with 2 hours of sudden tearing interscapular pain radiating to the lower back. His BP is 178/108 in the right arm and 174/106 in the left arm. There is no neurological deficit, no limb ischaemia and no mesenteric ischaemia features. A CT aortogram shows a dissection flap originating distal to the left subclavian artery extending into the proximal abdominal aorta, with no aortic rupture or visceral malperfusion. He has known hypertension on irregular ramipril.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 178/108, HR 92, RR 18, SpO2 98% on room air, Temp 36.8 degrees Celsius
data_table.Examination: Equal peripheral pulses, no neurological deficit, soft non-tender abdomen
data_table.Investigations: Troponin negative, lactate 1.4 mmol/L, creatinine 92 micromol/L; CT angiogram as described; ECG no ischaemic changes; chest X-ray widened mediastinum

lead_in: Which is the single most appropriate initial pharmacological priority?

options.text: Sublingual glyceryl trinitrate 600 micrograms followed by oral amlodipine 10 mg as the priority agent for blood pressure control in this presentation today
options.rationale: Vasodilators alone can reflex-increase shear stress (dP/dt); rate control with a beta blocker must precede or accompany them. (Source: NHFA aortic syndromes statement)

options.text: Intravenous fluid loading with 1 litre of 0.9 percent saline followed by oral metoprolol tartrate 50 mg as initial management in this acute aortic syndrome presentation today
options.rationale: Fluid loading is inappropriate in hypertensive dissection and oral beta blockade is too slow for acute shear stress reduction. (Source: NHFA aortic syndromes statement)

options.text: Emergent transfer for open thoracotomy and graft repair of the descending aorta as the priority intervention for this uncomplicated type B aortic dissection patient today
options.rationale: Uncomplicated type B dissection is initially medical; surgery or TEVAR is reserved for malperfusion, rupture or refractory pain. (Source: NHFA aortic syndromes statement)

options.text: Intravenous unfractionated heparin loading at 80 units/kg then a continuous infusion to prevent dissection flap thrombosis and downstream ischaemia in this man today
options.rationale: Anticoagulation is contraindicated in acute aortic dissection because of haemorrhage and false lumen extension risk. (Source: NHFA aortic syndromes statement)

options.text: Intravenous esmolol or labetalol infusion titrated to heart rate under 60 and systolic BP under 120 mmHg, plus parenteral analgesia and urgent vascular surgery review
options.rationale: Acute aortic dissection management targets shear stress reduction with IV beta blockade first, BP under 120 systolic, vasodilator added second if needed. (Source: NHFA aortic syndromes statement)

explanation.summary: Acute aortic dissection management centres on reducing aortic wall shear stress (dP/dt). Intravenous beta blockade (esmolol or labetalol) is started first, targeting heart rate under 60 and systolic BP under 120 mmHg, with a vasodilator added if BP control is inadequate. Uncomplicated type B dissection is medically managed; complicated type B (malperfusion, rupture, refractory pain) is treated with TEVAR. Type A dissection requires urgent cardiothoracic surgery.
key_points: Target heart rate under 60 and systolic BP under 120 mmHg with IV beta blockade first
Vasodilators are added second after beta blockade to avoid reflex tachycardia and shear stress
Uncomplicated type B dissection is medically managed; complicated type B prompts TEVAR
explanation.pearls: Always start a beta blocker before any vasodilator in aortic dissection because pure vasodilator therapy causes reflex tachycardia and increased dP/dt, worsening shear stress on the dissection flap.
